tree_summary.text: More than the numbers — the real-life wins training makes possible.
A Path connects your training to what actually matters — evidence-backed goals for mental health, bone strength, brain function, and more.
Choose what matters to you. Select up to 2 paths.
Exercise is one of the most evidence-backed interventions for depression.
Resistance training builds brain, not just muscle.
Load your bones to keep them.
A little of everything, done well — strong, conditioned, and mobile.
Learn the craft of training, from the ground up.
Capacity over outcome. Hear and respond to your body's signals.
You know your goals. We'll get out of your way. Only recommended for experienced lifters who want to do their own programming.
Set up a program to see your week here.
No program enrolled yet. Head to Paths to get started.
More than the numbers — the real-life wins training makes possible.

Three macrocycles that walk a true beginner from movement literacy to self-programming: learn the patterns and effort (Foundations) → build real strength under load (Build) → peak, periodize, and take the wheel (Own It).

Two macrocycles that build on each other: grow by doing more work (Accumulation — Volume), then add heavier, tension-focused training (Tension — Mechanical). Each is its own arc of blocks ending in a deload, with a light active-rest week between them. ~25 weeks total.

Two macrocycles that build on each other: add the muscle that produces force (Base — Hypertrophy), then turn it into strength (Build — Strength). Each is its own arc of blocks ending in a deload, with a light active-rest week between them. ~25 weeks total.

A 2-macrocycle movement plan. Consistency is the goal, not load. The second macrocycle offers an optional switch to a different set of movements — a new way to move the body — at the same easy effort.

Two macrocycles that build on each other: grow by doing more work (Accumulation — Volume), then add heavier, tension-focused training (Tension — Mechanical). Each is its own arc of blocks ending in a deload, with a light active-rest week between them. ~25 weeks total.

Two macrocycles that build on each other: add the muscle that produces force (Base — Hypertrophy), then turn it into strength (Build — Strength). Each is its own arc of blocks ending in a deload, with a light active-rest week between them. ~25 weeks total.

Same Ground Up curriculum as the 3-day track, split across an upper/lower four-day week for more per-session volume: Foundations → Build — Strength → Own It — Autonomy, graduating to On my own journey.

A 2-macrocycle movement plan. Consistency is the goal, not load. The second macrocycle offers an optional switch to a different set of movements — a new way to move the body — at the same easy effort.

Two macrocycles that build on each other: grow by doing more work (Accumulation — Volume), then add heavier, tension-focused training (Tension — Mechanical). Each is its own arc of blocks ending in a deload, with a light active-rest week between them. ~25 weeks total.

Two macrocycles that build on each other: add the muscle that produces force (Base — Hypertrophy), then turn it into strength (Build — Strength). Each is its own arc of blocks ending in a deload, with a light active-rest week between them. ~25 weeks total.
The first step in your journey. Learn the fundamental compound and isolation movements while establishing your starting weights. Focus is 100% on form and muscle activation, not load.

Develop the skill of Reps in Reserve (RIR) perception. Through a 8-week curriculum, you'll learn to accurately estimate how much effort you have left in the tank, enabling precise autoregulation for life.
Log cardio or other activity so it counts toward your week and your strength readiness. We never estimate calories.

Take a few minutes to come down.
Your muscles are warmest right now — gentle movement and stretching settle the best while it's fresh.
A few easy minutes of walking eases your heart rate down and keeps blood from pooling.
In 4, hold 4, out 4, hold 4 — settles your heart rate and your head.
Optional, and the best time for it — hold 30–60s for the muscles you trained.
Child's pose → cat-cow → downward dog → pigeon. Slow and easy.
One slow song — gentle active recovery that doubles as a mood moment.

Tilt the ear toward the shoulder, letting the opposite shoulder relax down. Hold 20–30s per side — never force it.
